model_narrative: We run a paper mill with several machines that can each produce several grades of paper. For every grade we know its monthly demand, its selling price, and on each machine its production rate and its production cost per ton. We decide how many tons of each grade to produce on each machine. The objective is to make the total monthly profit as large as possible.

sets.name: g
sets.doc: the grades of paper that can be produced

params.name: prate
params.doc: production rate of a grade on a machine, in tons per hour

params.name: pcost
params.doc: production cost of a grade on a machine, in dollars per ton

params.name: dempr_demand
params.doc: monthly demand for each grade that must be produced exactly, in tons per month

params.name: dempr_price
params.doc: selling price of each grade, in dollars per ton

params.name: avail
params.doc: available machine time on each machine, in hours per month

vars.name: outp
vars.doc: the tons of a grade produced on a machine per month

vars.name: profit
vars.doc: total monthly profit in dollars per month

constraints.name: outp[g,
constraints.intent: Each machine has only so many hours of running time available each month. The time a machine spends on a grade is the tons of that grade produced on it divided by how fast that grade runs on that machine. For each machine, the total running time it spends across all grades must not exceed the time available on that machine.

constraints.name: outp[g,
constraints.intent: Every grade has a fixed monthly demand that must be met exactly, no more and no less. For each grade, the total tons of it produced across all machines must equal its demand.

constraints.name: profit
constraints.intent: Total profit is the revenue from selling every grade at its demand quantity times its price, minus the cost of producing each grade on each machine, summed over all grades and machines. Set the profit variable equal to that net amount.

query: There's a proposal on the table to cap machine-3's monthly throughput at 20000 tons so we can free it up for a trial coating line. If we hold machine-3 to 20000 tons total, what does the optimizer return for profit?
expected_pyomo: model.m3_throughput_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.outp[g, 'machine-3'] for g in model.g) <= 20000)

query: We're evaluating a continuity-of-supply rule for tissue-wrp: keep at least 2400 tons of it running off machine-1 each month, so a machine-1 outage can't strand the whole tissue order. Plug that in and tell me where profit lands.

query: The current plan is dumping 25-bond-wt entirely onto machine-2 and c-bond-ext entirely onto machine-3. Couldn't we require that no single machine handles more than 60% of any one grade's monthly tonnage? Shouldn't a more spread-out schedule still be competitive on profit?

query: Production looks lopsided — most of the grade-machine grid sits idle while three or four cells carry the whole load. Couldn't we require every machine to produce at least 20% of each grade's monthly demand? Shouldn't a more spread-out schedule still pull a workable profit?

query: Machine-1 is barely loaded while machine-2 is carrying the plant. Shouldn't every machine be pulling its weight — say, each one running at least a quarter of total tonnage? What's keeping the model from balancing the lines out like that?

query: Machine-2 is hogging the schedule — it's running close to half the whole plant's output. Wouldn't it be healthier to keep any single machine under 40% of total tonnage so we're not so exposed if it goes down? What's the model seeing that makes it lean so hard on one line?
